text: I usually have some toast with butter and jam, maybe some tea, and perhaps an egg, either fried or scrambled. If I'm feeling fancy, I might make myself a bowl of oatmeal with fruit and a bit of honey. Nothing too elaborate, really, just something simple and comforting to start the day off right. I find that a leisurely morning is essential, so I like my breakfast to be easy to prepare while still giving me plenty of time to read the paper or catch up on some correspondence before starting work.

I usually have a sandwich or leftovers from last night's dinner, accompanied by a cuppa tea, in my dining room while watching the news or a documentary on telly. Occasionally, I might meet up with some colleagues or friends at a nearby café or pub for a more leisurely meal. They always chat quietly and we tend to avoid discussing politics unless someone starts it! Most weeks, though, I'm happy to eat at home where I can take my time and enjoy my food in peace.
I'm a bit of an early bird, so I tend to be in bed by 10 o'clock most nights. Getting enough rest is essential for me, especially considering my morning productivity peaks between 8 am and noon.

Self-care is rather more than just taking a relaxing bath or getting enough sleep; it's being mindful of one's overall wellbeing. For me, having regular exercise helps – though I'm not exactly what you'd call an athlete! A brisk walk outside during my morning stroll gets some fresh air into my lungs and sets me up for the rest of the day. I find journaling before bed really helpful too. It clears out any thoughts or worries I might have accumulated throughout the day, allowing me to focus on the positives. My late husband used to say I was always better prepared because of it. Now I make sure to write down everything so I remember all those little reminders and notes he left me. Writing has been incredibly comforting since his passing. Occasionally, treating myself to something nice makes a difference too – like buying a new notebook or sipping tea while enjoying some freshly baked biscuits made by our grandkids. Simple pleasures bring joy, and we should never underestimate them! Lastly, keeping busy is essential for me. Engaging in hobbies like gardening or tending to my home feels satisfying. Even a bit of tidying away, rearranging books on my shelves... these everyday routines give me peace of mind, even if only for a little while. Doing something creative

I've always been quite focused on my work and responsibilities, so networking has never really been a priority for me. As a secretary, I was more likely to be helping others rather than looking out for myself. However, since retiring, I have made an effort to connect with former colleagues and acquaintances, mainly through informal gatherings like our monthly ladies' luncheon group and annual Christmas parties at the local village hall. Occasionally, we'll discuss shared experiences related to education or the industry, which helps maintain some connections. My son still works closely with his mum – occasionally he organises meetings between us when needed. It feels nice having these little threads keeping people connected even though we're all busy lives now.

I find having a dedicated space helps me focus better. My desk is near a window so I have some natural light, which really does make a difference. Plus, my computer is set up comfortably, with my chair and keyboard all adjusted just so. To be honest, one of the most helpful habits I've developed while working from home is taking regular breaks. Getting up every hour or two to stretch and move around makes sure I'm staying alert and refreshed. And making a list of tasks to complete each morning before starting work also helps me prioritize what needs to be done. It's quite simple, really – but sometimes those little tricks can go a long way!

I'm a bit fussy about video calls, to be honest. I mean, I know they're convenient and all that, but sometimes I feel like we lose something of the personal touch when we're talking face-to-face isn't an option. My son does them quite regularly because he lives far away, so I've gotten used to seeing his face every now and then, even though it is via screen. If someone asks me to have a video call, I'll usually agree unless there's some other way to catch up, like emailing or writing letters – those always seem more civilised to me! But generally speaking, once I settle into the chat, I suppose I adapt fairly well. It's still better than nothing, and I try my best to make conversation flow smoothly.

I think staying motivated is quite simple really. You see, before retirement, my work was a big part of my identity, so giving up that sense of purpose was a bit of an adjustment. But since retiring, I've found that having some sort of routine helps me stay focused. For me, it's getting up early – still have to be at the bedside alarm clock every morning! It gives me a feeling of accomplishment knowing I've had a productive start to the day, even though I'm only going out occasionally now. It's also lovely to have something to look forward to each day; whether it's tending to my garden, catching up with family, or simply enjoying a nice cuppa while watching the birds outside. And of course, being involved in various hobbies like gardening and reading helps too. Those things give me a sense of satisfaction and motivation, which carries me through those quieter moments. Plus, I try to remind myself why I wanted to retire in the first place – to spend more quality time with loved ones and pursue personal interests. So, all these little things add up, keeping me feeling pretty content and motivated.

You know, I think one of the most challenging aspects of being an admin role is adapting to shifting priorities. In my previous jobs, we had a very structured system, and our workload was fairly predictable. But once I moved into more senior positions, like running the GP surgery, I found myself juggling multiple competing demands all the time. To be honest, it took me some getting used to, but I learned to prioritize based on what needed immediate attention and delegate where necessary. I'd make a mental list – or sometimes even write it down – of everything that required action, and focus on tackling the most urgent tasks first. It helps knowing your team members have different areas of expertise and strengths; so, while I might take charge of overall coordination, others could tackle specific projects. Communication is key too – making sure everyone knows what's expected of them and how long they need to complete certain tasks. I must admit, there were still moments when things got overwhelming! But generally, managing multiple priorities has become second nature now. It requires flexibility, staying organised, and trusting your instincts...and having a cuppa nearby whenever you're feeling frazzled!

I think being assertive is key. As a former admin assistant and secretary, I was used to taking charge and managing multiple priorities, so I find it quite second nature now. In my current stage of retirement, I like to have open conversations with people I interact with, whether it's my grandkids, friends, or even strangers who need assistance. When necessary, I'm prepared to politely express my thoughts and opinions while still showing respect for others' views. If someone asks me what I'd like done, say or how something should be improved, I'll usually explain why and provide examples where applicable. For instance, if there's an issue with a service or product, I might calmly point out areas for improvement – using specific instances rather than simply stating complaints. It's all about finding common ground and contributing meaningfully. My motto has always been "get your facts straight" before expressing concerns; once you're informed and grounded, you become more effective in advocating for yourself.

Well, I think most people have had moments where they feel like they're being misinterpreted or not understood properly. For me, the key is taking a deep breath and asking myself what really happened there. What was said or done that might be causing the misunderstanding? Sometimes we say something before we even mean to, or we assume someone else has picked up on certain cues that aren't actually there. I find it helpful to clarify the situation directly with the other person involved, rather than letting it fester. A simple "Let me explain" or "That wasn't my intention" can go a long way in clearing up any confusion. And sometimes, it takes a bit more effort, but having an open conversation can work wonders in resolving misunderstandings. It's also worth remembering that everyone gets things wrong sometimes – it's all part of human fallibility! My husband used to always say, "There's only one thing worse than getting something wrong: never explaining why." So, I try to take the time to explain the reasoning behind my actions, which usually helps prevent future misunderstandings. It's easier to ask forgiveness than to let resentment build up, isn't it?

I love spending quality time with my grandkids! They're such a joy, always full of energy and curiosity. We usually have lovely chats while we bake together, play games, or watch a film. My daughter is an excellent cook, so we always end up having delicious meals together. It's wonderful to see them grow and hear about what's going on in their lives. Sometimes, I'll share stories from my past, like my husband and I were married before he...passed away. The kids find those interesting and ask lots of questions. Family gatherings make me feel grateful to be surrounded by these precious people who bring so much happiness into my life.

I'm not really sure what all the fuss is about, to be honest. As a former secretary and administrator, I didn't exactly have much need for social media platforms like LinkedIn during my working days. My professional network was largely built up informally through word-of-mouth and attending local networking events. Nowadays, I find myself struggling to keep track of who's posting what on those sorts of websites...and why anyone feels the need to share so many personal details! Give me a cuppa and a chat with someone face-to-face any day.

I think sensitivity is key when discussing difficult subjects with people. It really depends on who we're talking to, though. With close family members like my grandkids, I'm very open and honest, even if it might be uncomfortable at first. But with colleagues or acquaintances, I try to tread more carefully, making sure not to upset them unnecessarily. And of course, there are certain issues – like politics or personal health concerns – where a bit of tact is essential. A straight forward but gentle approach usually works best for me.

Peer pressure isn't something I really think much about these days. As someone who's always been quite determined and set in my ways, I find myself standing out a bit among others who might be more inclined to follow the crowd. Of course, there have been instances where I'd rather go against what everyone else is doing, simply because I believe it's the right thing to do, even if it goes against popular opinion. When younger people come along, though – like my grandkids, for example – they try to persuade me to join them in certain activities or behaviors that aren't exactly up my alley. And while I love spending quality time with them, we usually talk things through until we reach an agreement that suits us both. It's all about finding common ground, you know? I suppose one way you could say I'm resistant to peer pressure is that I tend to trust my instincts and listen more to internal guidance rather than external influences. But that doesn't mean I never consider other viewpoints; far from it! When faced with difficult decisions, I weigh options thoughtfully before making a choice, taking into account personal preferences and long-term consequences. Ultimately, being open-minded yet resolute has helped me navigate those tricky scenarios pretty effectively over the years.

I try to be quite wary of them, to be honest. They always seem so tailored to my supposed preferences, even though I'm sure they're guessing! It makes me feel like they know far too much about me already. I'll usually ignore them unless something catches my eye – maybe a sale or promotion that might interest someone else rather than myself. But generally, I think they're more annoying than helpful.

To be honest, I'm still figuring out how to manage all these new-fangled web pages properly! My grandkids say it's a bit too complicated for me. They're always showing me how to close unnecessary windows and whatnot. But usually, I end up closing them one by one until only the ones I need are left open – simple enough! Sometimes I find myself clicking away at icons until everything gets jumbled again... must admit, getting more frustrated every time my brain starts feeling muddled like an unsorted laundry basket full of socks! Still, managing multiple tabs isn't something I spend much time worrying about; mainly because there aren't many I really need to have open at any given moment. Give me a quiet cuppa and I'll happily sit here sorting papers and emails instead!

I try to ignore them whenever possible. As a matter of fact, my late husband used to say that ignoring bullies was one way to starve them of the reaction they're looking for. It's quite simple, really – if we give them attention, they'll continue to shout; if we ignore them, eventually they fade away. Of course, there have been instances where I'd like to respond, perhaps offer some sense, but ultimately, I believe that engaging with someone who only wants to provoke is unlikely to achieve any worthwhile outcome.

Well, my grandchild usually sorts me out, bless him! He's very handy with those gadgets. If he's free, I'll ask him to come round and sort everything out. Otherwise, we have a chat and try to troubleshoot together via phone while he guides me step-by-step. It's quite alright, actually – we manage to resolve most issues between us. Sometimes, though, I think to myself, "Why didn't I take up computing lessons when I had the chance?" But overall, having someone to rely on helps a lot.

I'm quite diligent about keeping track of my finances and making sure all bills are paid on time. I write down every single payment due date and amount in a big notebook, which helps me keep everything straight. Then, I set reminders on my trusty calendar, so I never forget. My husband used to be very involved too, but sadly he passed away six years now. Still, we'd always worked together like clockwork, dividing up our expenses and whatnot. Now, it's just me taking charge. I find having something tangible – pen and paper – really works better for me rather than relying solely on digital tools; there's just fewer distractions! Plus, being methodical and seeing everything laid out clearly makes sense of it all for me. It feels more secure knowing exactly how much is going into each account and when payments need to happen.

I think about whether I'll actually end up using something before deciding what to cancel. It sounds silly, but I figure why pay for something I'm unlikely to make much use of. If I haven't looked at a magazine subscription in months, chances are I never will again. Same goes for streaming services – if I find myself skipping episodes left and right because there aren't enough shows we're interested in watching together, maybe one or two should go. My grandkids always say, "Nana, how many magazines have you got?" And I reckon they'd be surprised! But seriously, it's all about weighing convenience against cost; sometimes cutting ties helps me save some money, which is nice for putting towards other stuff... like my grandbabies coming to visit!

I'd say I'm a very deliberate buyer when it comes to making major decisions like replacing my old washing machine. Before buying something, I take some time to research, weigh up options, consider what features are really necessary, and think about how much it'll fit into our budget. It's all about finding a balance between getting what we need now versus breaking the bank. Once I feel fairly certain which one is best for us, I make an informed decision and stick to my priorities – nothing too extravagant!

I think it's all about weighing up the pros and cons, isn't it? When I'm deciding whether to buy something new or second-hand, I usually start by considering what will last longer and save me money in the long run. If someone has clearly taken proper care of an item before selling it, a pre-loved one is probably better value. But if it's been neglected or shows signs of wear and tear, I'll lean towards getting a brand-new one. For example, my old washing machine was nearing its end-of-life, so replacing it with a newer model seemed like the sensible choice. On the other hand, I still have some decent furniture pieces that my late husband made – those I'd rather keep using them until they're truly worn out. It comes down to making informed decisions based on each individual situation.

I believe it all comes down to being mindful of one's spending habits and sticking to a strict budget. My husband used to say "a penny saved is a penny earned" and his mum always taught me to be frugal – waste nothing, spend wisely. It's simple really, make a list of essential expenses first, then work out how much disposable income we have left. We shop smart during sales, cut corners where necessary, and never take out credit cards unnecessarily. I find writing everything down helps too; makes us see what we're actually getting into. Plus, having enough money put aside each month gives peace of mind. It's hard sometimes, especially when grandkids ask for something big like an Xbox! But we save up together as a team until they can afford it themselves. Always been like that since my husband was away serving in the Forces – taught him the importance of living below our means while he was deployed.

I love a bargain! When I see those letters in the paper saying there's a sale, my first thought is "oh, how many nice bits of china and clothes can we afford?" My late husband used to say I was like a magpie, always eyeing up something shiny, even if it wasn't quite what I needed. Anyway, during the winter months, I try to take advantage of them whenever I can – have some sort of treat for myself or perhaps buy gifts for the grandkids. It's lovely getting into the spirit of things, isn't it? And besides, all that extra cash does go towards our joint fund for travel... which will hopefully be bigger now that the weather improves.

Well, you see, portion control is all about being mindful of what we eat, isn't it? As a child, my mum always said "use up every scrap" – never waste food! It taught me to be thrifty with our rations during wartime, which stuck with me throughout my life. Now, I find myself still watching out how much I'm eating, even though I have more opportunities now. I try to fill half my plate with veggies, a quarter with protein, and save room for a bit of pudding. And, of course, I make sure to drink plenty of water between meals. My late husband used to tease me about making him feel like he was starving most of the time because I'd insist on using only one serving size for each item! Still, I believe moderation is key; too little and you might end up wanting everything, while too much and... well, let's avoid that altogether, shall we?

I think a more traditional talk-through sort of thing is best for me. You know, sit down, have a cuppa, and really listen to what someone has to say. None of those fancy therapies where you're supposed to be talking about your feelings all the time. For me, getting something off my chest usually does the trick. It's like clearing out a cluttered room – once everything's sorted, I feel much better. Plus, I'm not one for beating myself up over things; I believe we all make mistakes, and it's how we learn from them that counts. A friendly ear and some sensible advice go a long way, too.

I think preventing burnout is all about striking a balance between work and rest, you know. For me, having set routines helps - like my morning walk before breakfast, followed by some reading and tidying up the garden. And making sure I take breaks during the day to stretch and move around. It's funny how even though I'm retired now, those habits formed so many years ago while I was working still stick! Having something to look forward to each day, whether it's a visit from one of my grandkids or a nice cuppa with a neighbour, really makes all the difference. Plus, keeping busy with hobbies like gardening and reading keeps my mind occupied and energised. Of course, taking time out altogether isn't always easy, but knowing I have people who love me will make up for lost time whenever we catch up.

Well, I think a good night's sleep is essential, isn't it? For me, getting ready for bed is quite crucial – winding down with some calming tea, maybe a nice cupcake my grandkids baked, and then settling into something pleasant like a history novel before lights out. Keeping the room cool and dark helps too, of course. And avoiding screens beforehand is key; all those blue lights give me trouble falling asleep. I'd say establishing a regular bedtime routine has been helpful. It might sound dull, but consistency really does make a difference. My son always says I'm up bright and early, so it seems to be working! A bit more restful nights should mean better overall health, now that we're in our golden years.

I try to mix up my daily walk quite a bit, so I'm getting some fresh air and exploring different areas every now and then. You know, it's nice to see what else is going on in our neighbourhood while keeping myself fit. Occasionally, I'll join one of those local walking groups that meet at the park – we usually chat about books or current affairs during the stroll. And, of course, whenever my grandkids come to visit, we always take long walks together; that's great fun! As for more vigorous activity, I used to be able to manage a brisk pace, but since my knees aren't what they used to be... I find shorter bursts of faster walking suit me better these days. Still gets the heart rate up without putting too much strain on joints.

I like to think about why something will work best for me, rather than just looking at pros and cons. It helps me make a decision based on my priorities and what's most important. If there's too much jargon or complicated language, I tend to find myself getting bogged down and lose sight of the main point. But if someone explains it clearly and shows how it'll fit into my daily life, I'm more likely to be convinced. And, of course, having some idea about any past successes or failures helps – I'd always say that was an invaluable lesson!

I think incorporating feedback is absolutely crucial in refining a solution. When someone takes the time to provide constructive feedback, it shows there's been thought given to my work and they're invested in its success. For me, it's essential to take a step back, acknowledge what's being said, and assess whether those concerns are valid. If so, I make sure to listen actively and consider alternative approaches before implementing any changes. Sometimes, it might require tweaking an existing plan or starting anew altogether. What really helps is having open lines of communication – making space for discussion and allowing myself and others involved to share thoughts and feelings. It allows us to build trust and ensures we arrive at a mutually agreeable outcome.

I think my approach is quite methodical, really. I like breaking down complex issues into smaller parts, examining each component individually before looking at how they fit together. It helps me understand what's going on and makes sense of all the information. Sometimes, taking a step back and considering different angles is essential; it allows me to see patterns or connections I might have missed otherwise. Of course, there's always room for error, so I try to be open to adjusting my thoughts based on new insights or evidence – after all, that's where growth happens!

I find that taking a step back, breaking down complex information into smaller bits, and making notes really helps me grasp what's going on. Sometimes, I'll read something twice before moving on – once to catch the main idea initially, and again later to fill in any gaps. It might sound simple, but sometimes that extra bit of effort makes all the difference in getting my bearings.

I think identifying areas for growth is quite a personal process, isn't it? For me, it starts with being reflective about my strengths and weaknesses. When was the last time I had one of those 'eureka moments', where everything suddenly clicked into place? You know, like looking back on past achievements and wondering what led up to them? When I'm chatting with others, whether it's colleagues or grandkids, I try to listen attentively to how people describe themselves – what makes them tick, what they struggle with... And sometimes we find common ground, which helps spark some introspection myself. As an avid reader, I love getting lost in stories and histories, even though mine aren't always directly applicable! But there's something about immersing oneself in someone else's journey that gives insight into our shared human struggles and triumphs. Plus, authors often pose thought-provoking questions, encouraging us to reflect on our own lives. For instance, recently re-reading a biography reminded me of how much I value kindness, compassion, and hard work. Those qualities have been essential throughout my career, yet I'd be lying if I said I hadn't fallen victim to frustration and doubt along the way. What's interesting is how these lessons can resurface whenever I

I think my approach is quite simple, really. You see, I've found that being a bit of an ambivert has its advantages – I'm comfortable enough in social situations, but I still appreciate some quiet time on my own. When faced with something new, I try to break it down into smaller, manageable parts, like taking a walk or having a cuppa before diving in. Sometimes, that helps me gain clarity on what I need to focus on first. And, of course, talking it out with someone who knows me well usually does wonders! But ultimately, I suppose one must be open to making mistakes – after all, as the saying goes, "patience and curiosity" take us places we never thought we'd go!

I make sure to prioritize my downtime, like first thing in the morning before getting ready for the day, or during my lunch break. Even a bit of reading gets me going and sets a positive tone for the rest of the day. Plus, some days might be quieter than others, so having something to look forward to is nice. My grandkids sometimes join me too – we have lovely chats while I'm reading aloud to them or vice versa. It's wonderful quality time together!

I find out about new authors or genres mainly through word-of-mouth, recommendations from trusted sources like my book club or friends who share similar tastes. Sometimes I'll stumble upon a title while browsing in libraries or second-hand bookshops, which is always exciting! But generally speaking, I rely on people close to me suggesting books they think I'd enjoy – they usually know what kind of stories resonate with me best. Occasionally, I might pick up something recommended on the radio or TV shows; though, those aren't quite the same as getting an insider's tip-off from someone dear to me.

I find myself humming along to the tune most of the time! Occasionally, I'll try to sing a bit, although my singing voice is probably more suited to being ignored rather than appreciated. But yes, music seems like the perfect accompaniment to getting some work done – whether it's sorting papers, tidying up, or even knitting. It helps me focus, I think. Although sometimes, if I'm feeling nostalgic, I might put on something by Frank Sinatra or Vera Lynn and reminisce about the past. Music always brings out those memories quite vividly!

I must admit, I'm a bit of an old-fashioned listener, so I prefer my books in print. There's something about turning pages and having control over the pace that just feels more engaging to me. However, I have been known to listen to the radio while cooking or during a walk – it's quite pleasant. But if someone were to lend me one of those new-fangled e-readers or tablets, I'd give them a go! Just hope they're easy enough to navigate, mind...

I adore staying active, even though my energy levels aren't what they used to be! You know, getting older isn't all bad – I find that having a purpose and enjoying some exercise really helps me feel lively. My absolute favourite way to spend time is tending to my garden. There's something so satisfying about watching those seeds grow into beautiful vegetables and lovely flowers. It's meditative, too, and gets me outside, which does wonders for my mental health. In addition to that, I love taking long walks round our neighbourhood – we're lucky to have such nice scenery here. A gentle stroll usually gives me enough oomph to tackle whatever the rest of the day brings. And of course, there's always a bit of baking involved – whipping up a batch of scones or a cake for the grandkids is another great way to keep myself occupied. They always appreciate them, you see! Occasionally, if the weather permits, I'll join one of the local gardening clubs we have nearby. We meet once a month and share tips, advice, and gossip – it's become quite the highlight of my calendar! On occasion, I might treat myself to an afternoon tea class or two; it's lovely to mix with other folks who share similar interests

I thoroughly enjoy my time alone, actually. There's something so comforting about having some space to myself where I can think, read a book, tend to my garden... You know, just unwind without any distractions. It's lovely being able to put the kettle on, make a cuppa, sit down with a good novel, and simply relax. Of course, now that I'm widowed, spending quality time with my grandkids is precious too, but even that feels like me-time because we always have such wonderful conversations together. Still, there's nothing quite like curling up with a good book or a puzzle on a lazy Sunday morning – pure bliss!

I think the key is to be open-minded and give them a try. You never know what hidden gems you might discover! I love experimenting with different flavors and ingredients, even if some dishes turn out not quite to my taste. My late husband always said I was adventurous in food, which isn't entirely true – he'd rather stick to his roast chicken every Sunday – but I suppose we balanced each other out. If I'm feeling really unsure, I'll ask someone who knows more about the cuisine for guidance, like one of our grandkids who loves cooking Italian or Indian food. They're usually happy to share recipes and tips, and before long, I'm whipping up something delicious despite myself! Sometimes I take comfort in sticking to classic combinations and familiar spices; there's nothing wrong with that either. But trying new foods helps me feel like I'm staying young and experiencing all the world has to offer, even if only in a tiny way.

I think that's a great way to live life, having some variety in our free time is really important to me. You know, my husband used to say that we should never be bored because there was always something to look forward to. And now, since he's been gone, I try to stick to his idea. For me, most of my favourite things to do involve being outdoors – whether it's tending to my vegetable patch in the garden, taking long walks along the nearby canal, or simply sitting in my little garden enjoying nature. There's nothing like getting out into fresh air and watching the world go by, isn't there? But I'm also quite fond of reading, which gives me plenty of variety too! Whether it's curling up with a cuppa and a good historical novel, or dipping into a biography about someone fascinating, I love losing myself in another story. Occasionally, I'll treat myself to an audiobook while putting the kettle on – I find I can multitask rather nicely! And, of course, spending time with loved ones is essential. My grandkids come round all the time, and we have such lovely times together, playing games, sharing stories... those moments make everything worthwhile. Plus, sometimes we'll pop down to the

I'm quite particular about keeping track of my free time, isn't everyone? For me, a planner is still one of the best tools. I have a lovely A5 notebook where I jot down everything from what we're having for dinner to our grandkids' schedules – you know how it goes! It helps me stay organised and ensures I don't forget any important dates or appointments. My late husband used to tease me about being a bit too tidy, but I like knowing exactly what's going on whenever I glance at my diary. Of course, I also make sure to update the family calendar so everyone knows each other's plans ahead of time. Old-fashioned, perhaps, but it works for us!

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned girl, still like keeping a notebook and pen handy for jotting down reminders and notes! But seriously, I find that breaking things down into smaller chunks and creating routines helps me stay organised. For example, every morning before my peak productivity period starts, I make sure to plan out what I need to accomplish that day, including any tasks related to work, personal stuff, and errands. My calendar is filled up with everything, so I know exactly what's coming up and can avoid double bookings. My son always says I have an iron stomach, being able to juggle multiple things at once! As long as I stick to my system, nothing seems too much trouble – even though some days might be more chaotic than others due to fluctuations in my energy levels throughout the day.

I like to start my morning with a cup of tea and perhaps some gentle stretching exercises while still getting dressed. It helps loosen me up a bit. Then, I usually catch up on the news on the radio or watch the telly for a bit – something calming to ease into the day. Sometimes, if I'm feeling energetic, I might take a quick walk round our little garden to let the fresh air in and get myself going properly. Before heading out, I double-check my bag has everything I need, including my handbag, keys, and any other essentials. Off I go!

I'm quite keen on reducing our carbon footprint, even though we're not exactly a large household anymore what with my husband being gone. We still like having some warmth and comfort, so I make sure to turn down the heating whenever I can, using thick curtains during winter to keep out drafts, and opening them up during summer to let in the sunshine. I also try to cook meals that use minimal oil and gas – lots of vegetables and legumes are perfect for that! My grandkids love helping me plan menus and prep ingredients, too. And I must say, since retiring, I've been able to spend more time tending to our little veggie patch in the backyard; there's nothing better than growing one's own herbs and veggies. Simple measures add up, don't they?

I must admit, I find those smart devices a bit overwhelming. My son is always trying to convince me to upgrade my router or install voice assistants, but honestly, I'm happy with what I have. It's all a bit too complicated for an older person like myself. I mean, I know some people love them, but I'd rather spend my free time doing something else – perhaps tending to my garden or reading a good book. If I need assistance, I'll ask my grandkids to set up whatever device it might be. They're usually more tech-savvy than me!

Well, for me, prioritising is quite a matter of weighing up what's most important. It's all about getting down to basics and considering long-term consequences. When faced with multiple demands vying for my attention, I tend to focus first on essential tasks – like keeping track of bills, managing finances, and ensuring I have enough food and heating. Those fundamental necessities take precedence. After those essentials are covered, I look at bigger-picture objectives, such as spending quality time with loved ones, pursuing hobbies, or staying informed about current affairs. Of course, these priorities may shift slightly based on circumstances, but generally speaking, I try to strike a balance between what I need versus what brings me joy or satisfaction. It's worth noting that I'm rather particular about how I achieve those goals; once set upon them, I prefer to see them through systematically, taking deliberate steps towards achieving success. A bit too much chaos wouldn't suit me very well! Ultimately, finding an equilibrium between competing demands helps maintain stability in my daily life.
